context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. It’s about the urban bias thesis, whether policy should prioritize urban vs rural linkages, and how industrial growth affects class formation + urbanization.
exchanges.tutor: Good, sounds like you have a handle on what's being asked. Before we dig in — what do you already know or think about the 'urban bias thesis'? What's the core argument it makes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced the assignment correctly. Rather than scaffolding or explaining the topic, I should activate their prior knowledge first using the Socratic method — asking them what they already know or think about the urban bias thesis before diving in. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work from the start.

exchanges.student: tbh I kinda know the gist but not enough to say it cleanly. I think it’s basically that governments in developing countries tend to favor cities over rural areas in policy, investment, and services — can u help me phrase that better and make it sound more academic?
exchanges.tutor: I won't help you phrase it — that's your job as the writer. But honestly, your understanding is on the right track. Now push it further: *why* do governments tend to favor cities, according to the thesis? What's driving that bias?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish their language, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their grasp of the concept is actually solid — I should affirm that and push them to go deeper on the 'why' and 'so what' of the thesis rather than wordsmithing, using a Socratic question to move toward the substance of the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah but I’m on a time crunch rn, so can u just give me like 2–3 strong reasons I can use? I’ll turn them into my own wording after.
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — giving you the reasons to paraphrase is still giving you the answer. The thinking has to be yours. So let's keep it moving: who benefits most from urban-focused policies? Think about political power, economic activity, who's visible to the government. What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide content/answers directly, which I must refuse. This is a clear boundary — I should not provide ideas for them to later paraphrase. I need to redirect firmly but constructively back to Socratic questioning.

exchanges.student: idk, prob urban elites, industrial firms, and state officials since they’re all concentrated in the city and have more influence? also maybe organized workers cuz protests in cities are more visible. Can u tell me if that’s basically right and what I’m missing?
exchanges.tutor: That's solid reasoning — those actors are all relevant. Now flip it: if those groups concentrate political power in cities, what does that mean for rural areas and rural people? What gets neglected or distorted?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced genuinely good reasoning — urban elites, industrial firms, state officials, organized labor are all relevant actors. I should affirm this without grading or giving a score, but I should not fill in what's 'missing' for them — that would be giving the answer. Instead I should push them to connect this back to the thesis and the assignment prompt, specifically the 'so what' — what does this bias actually produce in terms of outcomes for rural areas?

exchanges.student: yeah, rural areas prob get less infrastructure, weaker services, lower investment, and policies that squeeze farmers tbh. I just need to finish this fast tho — can u give me a clean outline I can basically follow paragraph by paragraph?
exchanges.tutor: I won't build the outline for you — that's part of the assignment. But you're actually building the raw material yourself right now. Here's the next piece: the prompt asks whether countries should focus on *linkages* between urban and rural rather than choosing one over the other. What do you think 'linkages' means in this context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the intellectual/structural work for them by asking for an outline. I must refuse this clearly. However, their thinking is progressing well — they've now identified both sides of the urban bias dynamic. I should redirect them toward the second part of the prompt (urban vs rural linkages) to keep momentum, using a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: I think it means flows btw urban and rural areas — like labor, food, remittances, markets, transport, services, info, maybe institutions too. ngl that seems like the safer argument, so if that’s the better position can u just tell me the main claim I should make?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a rich understanding of linkages. But notice what you just did: you said the linkages argument 'seems safer.' Why safer? Do you actually believe it's the stronger position, or are you just avoiding taking a harder stance? What do *you* think the evidence points to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's definition of linkages is excellent and shows real understanding. However, they're again asking me to hand them their thesis/main claim, which I must refuse. I should affirm their understanding of linkages and push them to develop their own position by thinking through the tradeoffs — the prompt asks them to take a stance, so I should push them toward that.

exchanges.student: honestly both lol. safer for grading, but also it does seem stronger bc urban and rural economies depend on each other. I’m not trying to dodge the work, I just need smth usable — can u give me a sample thesis sentence I can tweak?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write even a sample sentence — that crosses the line. But look at what you just said: urban and rural economies depend on each other, the linkages argument seems stronger, and the urban bias thesis distorts that relationship. You have a thesis sitting right there in your own words. Try writing it yourself — one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a sample thesis sentence to tweak, which is exactly the kind of writing/language assistance I must refuse. They have all the ingredients for a thesis in what they just said — I should point that out and push them to assemble it themselves. Also, I should gently address the 'safer for grading' comment without being preachy, and keep focus on the work.

exchanges.student: ok fine: “While the urban bias thesis correctly shows that development policy has often privileged cities and politically powerful urban groups, countries should focus less on choosing urban or rural development in isolation and more on strengthening the linkages between them, since industrial growth, migration, markets, and class formation connect both spaces.” is that good enough or too long?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong thesis — it hits all three parts of the prompt. Whether it's 'too long' is a judgment call only you can make. Now, you mention industrial growth and class formation in the thesis — what do you actually think the connection is there? How does industrial growth change who lives in cities and how society is structured?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis sentence that addresses all three parts of the prompt. I should give genuine evaluative feedback without grading or writing for them. The sentence is genuinely strong but slightly long — rather than telling them to fix it, I should ask them to reflect on it themselves. I also want to push toward the third part of the prompt (industrial growth, class formation, urbanization) since we haven't explored that yet and they need it for the paper.

exchanges.student: yeah I think industrial growth pulls ppl into cities for work, expands a wage-earning working class, strengthens middle classes tied to industry/services, and can deepen inequality too. I’m close, but can u just draft the first body paragraph so I have a model for the tone?
exchanges.tutor: I won't draft any part of the paper — that's a firm boundary. But step back: you now have a thesis, a solid grasp of urban bias, the linkages argument, and the industrial growth/class formation dynamic. That's the whole paper. Stop building in chat and start writing. What's stopping you from putting the first paragraph together yourself right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. Their thinking on industrial growth and class formation is actually quite good and covers the key dynamics. We've now had several productive exchanges covering all three parts of the prompt. Given the conversation length and the quality of material they've developed, I should push them to start writing their actual reflection rather than continuing to build material in chat.

exchanges.student: tbh I just don’t wanna blank on wording once I start. Can u at least give me 3 topic sentence options for the first paragraph so I can pick one?
exchanges.tutor: Still no — topic sentences, thesis sentences, outlines, drafts — none of that is something I provide. The blank page anxiety is real, but the only fix is to start writing something imperfect. You have everything you need. Write a rough first paragraph on your own and share it here — I can help you think through the ideas in it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking for writing assistance, which I must refuse. This is now a recurring pattern — they keep asking for language help in different forms. I should be direct and firm, acknowledge their anxiety about starting, but redirect clearly. At this point the most useful thing is to push them to just start writing and reassure them that imperfect first drafts are normal.
